sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 25-year-old male, presents with a 3-week history of severe, squeezing pain on the right-hand side of his belly, located underneath his ribs.
He describes the pain as an 8 out of 10 in severity and notes that it comes and goes, often accompanied by nausea and vomiting.
The pain is exacerbated by eating, particularly fatty foods, which is a staple of his current keto diet.
He has lost 25 pounds over the last 3 months since starting the diet and has seen improvement in his blood sugar control.
His past medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes, which he has managed with diet and exercise.
He has a family history of gallbladder disease, with both parents having had their gallbladders removed.
Review of systems: The patient reports occasional low-grade fevers and nausea, but no vomiting.
He denies any fever at the time of the visit.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure was 128/88, respiratory rate was 16, pulse rate was 68, and oxygen saturation on room air was 99%.
Physical exam: The patient has a regular heart rhythm and no murmurs, clicks, or rubs.
The lungs are clear and equal bilaterally.
The abdomen is soft and nondistended, with positive bowel sounds.
There is slight guarding in the right upper quadrant, but no rebound tenderness.
The patient experiences pain when pressure is applied to the right-hand side of his abdomen, consistent with Murphy's sign.
Test Results: Abdominal ultrasound shows multiple gallstones in the gallbladder, with mild thickening of the gallbladder wall.
The common bile duct size is within normal limits at 4.1 mm.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has mild cholecystitis with gallstones, with a possible obstruction not appreciated.
Problem list: Cholecystitis; Gallstones; Type 2 diabetes (status: confirmed); Family history of gallbladder disease.
Plan:
I prescribed [NOT MENTIONED] for [NOT MENTIONED].
I recommended a laparoscopic cholecystectomy to remove the gallbladder, with a surgery time of about an hour to an hour and a half.
The patient will spend the night in the hospital for monitoring post-procedure and will be advised to avoid high-fat foods and eat high-fiber foods after surgery.
The patient will be encouraged to get up and move around on solid ground within a day or two after surgery.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in a week to monitor his progress and release him to return to normal activities, including hiking and photography.
